title: Development and current status of the CHARMM force field for nucleic acids
abstract:
Development and Current
Status of the CHARMM Force
Field for Nucleic Acids
Alexander D. MacKerell, Jr.
Nilesh Banavali*
Nicolas Foloppe†
Department of Pharmaceutical
Sciences,
School of Pharmacy,
University of Maryland,
20 N. Pine Street,
Baltimore, MD 21201
Received 15 March 2001;
accepted 15 March 2001
Published online 18 December 2001
Abstract:
The CHARMM27 all-atom force ﬁeld for nucleic acids represents a highly optimized
model for investigations of nucleic acids via empirical force ﬁeld calculations. The force ﬁeld
satisfactorily treats the A, B, and Z forms of DNA as well as RNA, and it also useful for nucleosides
and nucleotides. In addition, it is compatible with the CHARMM force ﬁelds for proteins and lipids,
allowing for simulation studies of heterogeneous systems.
© 2001 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
Biopoly (Nucleic Acid Sci) 56: 257–265, 2001
Keywords:
CHARMM; force ﬁeld; molecular mechanics; empirical; molecular dynamics; DNA;
RNA
INTRODUCTION
Empirical force ﬁeld calculations on nucleic acids is
an approach that allows for structure–function rela-
tionships to be obtained at an atomic level of detail.1
Recently, the ﬁeld has matured to the state that mo-
lecular dynamics (MD) simulations can be performed
on oligonucleotides that are in satisfactory agreement
with a variety of experimental data.2 Below, we de-
scribe the CHARMM force ﬁeld for nucleic acids,
emphasizing the approaches used in its development,
the quality of the force ﬁeld for studies of nucleic
acids, and insights gained into the properties of DNA
and RNA obtained during the parametrization efforts.
DEVELOPMENT HISTORY
Initial theoretical studies of biological macromole-
cules based on empirical force ﬁelds were performed
in vacuum due to limitations in computer resources.
Accordingly, the original nucleic acid force ﬁeld as-
sociated with the program CHARMM,3,4 referred to
as EF2, was designed to be used in vacuo.5 Since
Correspondence to: Alexander D. MacKerell, Jr.; email:
alex@outerbanks.umaryland.edu
Contract grant sponsor: NIH
Contract grant number: GM51501
*Present address: Weill Medical College of Cornell University,

10021
†Present address: RiboTargets, Granta Park, Abington, Cam-
bridgeshire, CB1 6GB, UK
Biopolymers (Nucleic Acid Sciences), Vol. 56, 257–265 (2001)
© 2001 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
257
 simulation studies of biomolecules require represen-
tation of their aqueous environment, damping of elec-
trostatics due to the aqueous environment was in-
cluded implicitly in the force ﬁeld via a distance-
dependent
dielectric
constant
(i.e.,
where
the
dielectric constant is proportional to the distance be-
tween atoms). EF2 was adapted from an earlier pa-
rameter set6 that itself was put together from prior
force ﬁelds by Gelin and Karplus7 and Kollman et al.8
The force ﬁeld included both extended atom and
all-atom models. In the extended atom model the
nonpolar hydrogens are represented as being part of
“extended” nonhydrogen atoms to which they are
covalently bound while polar hydrogens are repre-
sented explicitly. The optimization of this force ﬁeld
concentrated primarily on the internal terms in the
potential energy function [see Eq. (1), below], based
on geometries, vibrational spectra, and conforma-
tional energetics. Nonbonded terms, including an ex-
plicit term for hydrogen bonds, were based on anal-
ogy with previously available parameters, and were
tested via crystal simulations and dimer interaction
energies. Application of this force ﬁeld to small DNA
duplexes showed them to be stable in MD simula-
tions, though the stability was strongly dependent on
the treatment of the dielectric constant.5
The next nucleic acid force ﬁeld in CHARMM was
designed to include an explicit representation of the
solvent,9 based on the TIP3P water model.10,11 This
force ﬁeld, referred to as the CHARMM22 nucleic
acid force ﬁeld, was an all-atom representation and
put emphasis on optimization of both the internal and
nonbonded terms. It should be emphasized that the
EF2 force ﬁeld5 included an explicit term for hydro-
gen bonds. Later work, however, showed that both the
angular and distance dependence of hydrogen bond-
ing could be adequately treated with only the electro-
static and Lennard–Jones (LJ) terms,11 leading to the
omission
of
the
hydrogen
bonding
term
in
CHARMM22 and subsequent force ﬁelds. Optimiza-
tion of the nonbonded parameters [i.e., terms for the
electrostatic and vdW or LJ terms, see Eq. (1), below]
was based on previously reported approaches11,12 and
emphasized compatibility with the CHARMM22
force ﬁelds for proteins13 and lipids.14 Charge distri-
butions for the electrostatic term were based on re-
production of quantum mechanics (QM) minimum
interaction energies and distances between model
compounds representing nucleic acid moieties and
water, or small molecule dimers [e.g., Watson–Crick
(WC) base pairs]. The van der Waals (VdW) or LJ
parameters were transferred from the proteins or
based on crystal heats of sublimation. This approach
yielded a force ﬁeld that properly balanced the inter-
action triad; the solute–solute, solute–solvent, and
solvent–solvent nonbond interactions. Proper balance
of the interaction triad is essential for MD simulations
in the condensed phase that yield accurate structural
and thermodynamic properties. This force ﬁeld has
been used in simulations of DNA,15–19 RNA,20 and
DNA– or RNA–protein complexes.21–24 However, it
was shown that MD simulations of DNA duplexes in
solution had a propensity to assume the A conforma-
tion25 in high water activity26–28; conditions where
the B form should be stable. These limitations moti-
vated development of the next generation CHARMM
nucleic acid force ﬁeld.
The most recent CHARMM force ﬁeld for nucleic
acids represents a complete reoptimization of the
CHARMM22 force ﬁeld and is referred to as the
CHARMM27 force ﬁeld for nucleic acids.29,30 In this
force ﬁeld special emphasis was placed on the non-
bonded and dihedral parameters. Nonbonded param-
eters were again optimized to be compatible with the
modiﬁed TIP3P water potential and were tested with
new parameters for sodium.31 More rigorous optimi-
zation of the nonbonded parameters, particularly the
charges, was performed to reproduce QM minimum
interaction energies and geometries and experimental
crystal data. For optimization of the dihedral param-
eters, new target data included extensive results from
QM calculations. These calculations involved the
conformational energetics about the rotatable bonds
of model compounds designed to mimic the phos-
phodiester backbone, the sugar, and the glycosyl link-
age.32–34 This information allowed for better optimi-
zation of the dihedral parameters associated with
those degrees of freedom. In addition, the dihedral
parameter optimization process included results from
MD simulations on multiple DNA and RNA duplex
structures for which experimental data is available
(see below). Those MD simulations primarily used
the PME approach (see below) to treat long-range
electrostatic interactions.35 These combined efforts
yielded a force ﬁeld that adequately treats DNA in
multiple environments including the equilibrium be-
tween the A and B forms as a function of water
activity.36,30 To date, the CHARMM27 nucleic acid
force ﬁeld has been used in MD studies on DNA,37
DNA–protein
complexes,38
and
DNA–lipid
sys-
tems.39
FUNCTIONAL FORM
The form of the potential energy function used in
CHARMM27 is shown below in Equation (1):

The most recent CHARMM force ﬁeld for nucleic
acids represents a complete reoptimization of the
CHARMM22 force ﬁeld and is referred to as the
CHARMM27 force ﬁeld for nucleic acids.29,30 In this
force ﬁeld special emphasis was placed on the non-
bonded and dihedral parameters. Nonbonded param-
eters were again optimized to be compatible with the
modiﬁed TIP3P water potential and were tested with
new parameters for sodium.31 More rigorous optimi-
zation of the nonbonded parameters, particularly the
charges, was performed to reproduce QM minimum
interaction energies and geometries and experimental
crystal data. For optimization of the dihedral param-
eters, new target data included extensive results from
QM calculations. These calculations involved the
conformational energetics about the rotatable bonds
of model compounds designed to mimic the phos-
phodiester backbone, the sugar, and the glycosyl link-
age.32–34 This information allowed for better optimi-
zation of the dihedral parameters associated with
those degrees of freedom. In addition, the dihedral
parameter optimization process included results from
MD simulations on multiple DNA and RNA duplex
structures for which experimental data is available
(see below). Those MD simulations primarily used
the PME approach (see below) to treat long-range
electrostatic interactions.35 These combined efforts
yielded a force ﬁeld that adequately treats DNA in
multiple environments including the equilibrium be-
tween the A and B forms as a function of water
activity.36,30 To date, the CHARMM27 nucleic acid
force ﬁeld has been used in MD studies on DNA,37
The form of the potential energy function used in
CHARMM27 is shown below in Equation (1):
MacKerell, Banavali, and Foloppe
In Eq. (1) Kb, K, KUB, K, and K are the bond,
valence angle, Urey–Bradley, dihedral angle, and im-
proper dihedral angle force constants, respectively; b,
, S, , and  are the bond length, bond angle,
Urey–Bradley 1,3 distance, dihedral torsion angle,
and improper dihedral angle, respectively. The sub-
script zero represents the equilibrium values for the
individual terms. In the dihedral term n is the multi-
plicity and  is the phase, which dictates the location
of the minima and maxima. The phase can be set to
any value in CHARMM and, combined with expan-
sion of the dihedral term into a Fourier series via the
multiplicity, allows for extended ﬂexibility in the di-
hedral term.40 These terms are referred to collectively
as the internal terms in the potential energy function.
Coulomb or electrostatic and vdW or LJ (6–12) terms
comprise the external or nonbonded interactions;   is
the LJ well-depth and Rmin is the distance at the LJ
minimum interaction energy; qi is the partial atomic
charge and  D is the dielectric constant; rij is the
distance between atoms i and j, respectively. For
explicit treatment of solvent  D is set to 1. Given the
positions, r, of the atoms, the various distances and
angles required to evaluate V(r) are readily deter-
mined. All possible bonds, valence angles and dihe-
dral angles are included in V(r), while a limited
number of Urey–Bradley terms and improper dihedral
angles are included to optimize the ﬁt to vibrational
spectra. Intramolecular nonbonded interactions be-
tween atoms attached by three (1–4 interactions) or
more covalent bonds are included in the energy cal-
culations and are not scaled.41 The LJ parameters
between different atom types are derived by use of the
arithmetic mean for Rmin and the geometric mean for
 . If required, explicit LJ parameters can be speciﬁed
Ideally, nonbonded interactions between all atoms
in the system should be treated explicitly, but such a
treatment is computationally expensive. Explicit treat-
ment of long-range interactions is especially impor-
tant for electrostatic interactions since they are pro-
portional to r1 as compared to the LJ interactions
which are proportional to r12 for the repulsive term
and r6 for the attractive term [Eq. (1)]. Traditionally,
the computational expense associated with treatment
of long-range interactions was minimized by the use
of atom truncation methods, where nonbonded inter-
actions between atoms are excluded beyond a speci-
ﬁed distance. Facilitating this approach in CHARMM
are atom-truncation smoothing schemes that avoid the
abrupt change in energies and forces at the truncation
distance.42 One way to avoid truncation problems
without using such atom-truncation smoothing meth-
ods is the use of the Ewald method, which takes
advantage of the fast convergence obtained by split-
ting the electrostatic interaction sum into direct space
and reciprocal space sums to treat all electrostatic
interactions in periodic systems.43 A recent grid-based
implementation of the Ewald method, particle mesh
Ewald (PME),35 minimizes the computational ex-
pense of the Ewald approach, making it of compara-
ble speed with atom truncation methods. An alterna-
tive approach is the fast-multipole method (FMM)44
where electrostatic interactions beyond a speciﬁed
cutoff are treated as multipole–multipole interactions.
CHARMM currently contains a selection of atom
truncation methods, Ewald, PME and a simpliﬁed
implementation of FMM45 allowing ﬂexibility in the
treatment of the nonbonded electrostatic interactions.
For the CHARMM27 nucleic acid ﬁeld, both PME
and atom truncation using the force-shifting method42
with a cutoff distance of 12 Å or more yield similar
results for DNA in solution with respect to both RMS
differences and ﬂuctuations, with the computational
expense being similar.30,37 Since PME theoretically
treats all long-range interactions, it is the recommended
approach for periodic boundary simulations while atom
truncation is appropriate for very large systems where
stochastic boundary methods must be used.46,31
PARAMETRIZATION PHILOSOPHY AND
Details of the optimization of the CHARMM27 nu-
cleic acid force ﬁeld, as well as the protein and lipid
force ﬁelds, has been presented elsewhere29,13,47,48;
only the philosophy behind the optimization process,
selected aspects of its implementation, and its rele-
vance to the nucleic acids will be covered in this
article. An important aspect of the CHARMM force
ﬁeld is extensive optimization of every term in the
force ﬁeld, with little emphasis on transferability. This
approach allows for maximum agreement with QM
target data for model compounds, and more impor-
CHARMM Nucleic Acids Force Field
tantly, experimental target data for model compounds
and macromolecules. However, this philosophy re-
quires that parameter development work be performed
when studying novel molecules. The required param-
eter development, however, can be readily performed
by following the published protocols48; example input
ﬁles to facilitate parameter development are available
on the web (see below). Moreover, the force ﬁeld has
already been extended to include a variety of “non-
standard” oligonucleotide moieties, as listed in Table I.
In the CHARMM27 nucleic acid force ﬁeld em-
phasis has been placed on balancing “local” small
molecule based properties with “global” condensed
phase or macromolecular properties during parameter
optimization. For example, with the nucleic acid
bases, charges and LJ parameters were optimized to
reproduce local properties including interactions of
the individual bases with water, Watson-Crick (WC)
and other base dimer hydrogen-bond interactions, and
base stacking interactions. In addition, parameter op-
timization also considered the global properties of
heats of sublimation and lattice constants of single
base crystals. This approach is also important for
proper treatment of the nonbonded interaction triad
mentioned above. Since empirical force ﬁeld calcula-
tions are often designed to obtain an atomistic picture
of events impacting experimentally observed proper-
ties, the proper balance of the local and global aspects
of the force ﬁeld is necessary to assure the quality of
the atomistic interpretation. Moreover, inclusion of a
maximal amount of local and global target data min-
imizes the parameter correlation problem, where mul-
tiple sets of parameters can yield similar properties.
Such an occurrence has been documented based on
the pure solvent properties of ethane.48
Another area of emphasis with the CHARMM27
nucleic acid force ﬁeld that relied on both local and
global target data was proper treatment of the confor-
mational energetics about the rotatable bonds in oli-
gonucleotides. Here the local/global balance was
based on potential energy surfaces on model com-
pounds and on condensed phase simulations of DNA,
respectively. The local data was obtained via QM
calculations on model compounds representing the
phosphodiester backbone dihedrals (	,
the sugar pucker (P and ) and the glycosyl linkage, 
(see Figure 2 of Ref. 29). The model compounds used
were designed to include all relevant functional
groups while still being small enough to allow for the
energetics associated with an individual dihedral to be
studied with minimal complicating contributions. QM
potential energy surfaces, typically calculated at the
MP2/6-31G(d) or MP2/6-31G(d) levels, were ob-
tained for a selected dihedral while other rotatable
bonds in the model compound were constrained to
values observed in the A or B forms of DNA based on
crystal survey data.34 This approach avoids problems
associated with the alternate dihedral degrees of free-
dom in the model compounds switching to different
minima during calculation of the dihedral potential
energy surface and assures that the focus of the pa-
rameter optimization is on regions of conformational
space relevant to biological systems of interest. In
addition, surfaces were done in the presence of both
the C3-endo and C2-endo sugar puckers25 due to
both puckers being found in nucleic acids and to
facilitate proper treatment of both the A and B forms
of DNA and of RNA by the force ﬁeld.
The CHARMM dihedral parameters were initially
optimized to reproduce the QM potential energy sur-
faces discussed in the preceding paragraph. It should
be emphasized that, whenever possible, this reproduc-
tion involved high-energy regions of the surfaces as
well as the energy minima. Proper treatment of the
high-energy regions is important as they can be sam-
pled in MD simulations and make the force ﬁeld
appropriate for calculations involving structural tran-
sitions [e.g., DNA stretching via potential of mean
force (PMF) calculations17]. Another relevant point in
the parameter optimization process is that a surface
cannot always be adjusted to give ideal agreement
with the QM target data (e.g., due to other adjust-
ments required to obtain agreement with the global
data, as discussed in the following paragraph). In such
Nonstandard Nucleic Acid Moieties for Which CHARMM
MacKerell, Banavali, and Foloppe
cases the surface is optimized to be softer (i.e., lower
energy barriers) than the QM target data. This empha-
sis minimizes the possibility that the force ﬁeld will
artiﬁcially restrict molecules to a given conformation.
Once the dihedral angle parameters were opti-
mized based on the local QM data, they were applied
in MD simulations of one or more DNA duplexes.
The behavior of the dihedrals in the simulations rep-
resents one aspect of the global data in the optimiza-
tion process. Dihedral probability distributions were
obtained from the simulations and compared with
distributions from crystal structures in the nucleic acid
database.49 Readjustment of the dihedral parameters
was then performed to take into account differences
between the crystal survey and calculated distribution,
while keeping the QM potential energy surfaces as a
reference. This was repeated in an iterative fashion
until agreement with the experimental crystal distri-
butions was deemed satisfactory. Such an approach
leads to deviations in the empirical potential energy
surfaces with respect to the local target data (i.e., the
QM surfaces), but yields better agreement with the
experimental, global target data. Since the force ﬁeld
is to be used in the condensed phase, emphasizing
agreement with the global, condensed phase experi-
mental data over the local data is an essential aspect of
the CHARMM parameter optimization procedure.
The need to deviate between the empirical and QM
surfaces may be related to several potential problems.
Possible limitations include the QM level of theory,
inappropriate model compounds, limited sampling in
the MD simulations, the assumption that the crystal
survey distributions are representative of DNA and
RNA in solution, and limitations in the form of the
potential energy function. Limitations in the QM level
of theory are possible but will probably not be a
dominant factor due to the use of MP250 to explicitly
treat electron correlation for the majority of calcula-
tions. Recent tests indicate that use of alternate basis
sets (e.g., Dunning’s correlation corrected sets51) or
treatment of electron correlation via MP3 do not sig-
niﬁcantly alter the energetics (N. Foloppe and A. D.
MacKerell, Jr., manuscript in preparation). During the
initial phases of the CHARMM27 parameter optimi-
zation, efforts were made to test the model com-
pounds to show that they gave results consistent with
experiment.32 This suggests that the model com-
pounds used are not a problem, though tests on alter-
nate model compounds are required to check this
more rigorously. As discussed above, comparison of
dihedral distributions from crystal surveys and MD
simulations were used, in part, to optimize the dihe-
dral parameters. An assumption in this approach is
that the MD simulations, typically 0.5–2 ns in dura-
tion, adequately sample the dihedral of interest. While
this assumption can only be tested with much longer
simulations, one test in which a simulation was ex-
tended from 2 to 5 ns did not show an increase in the
range of dihedrals sampled for the rotatable bonds in
DNA. The assumption that crystal survey data is
representative of duplex DNA and RNA in solution is
supported by the similarity between structures in the
two condensed phases,52,53 though this is qualiﬁed by
the limited resolution of NMR for elucidating the
structural properties of DNA in solution.54 Concern-
ing the form of the potential energy function, it has
been shown that class I force ﬁelds cannot accurately
reproduce both gas phase and condensed phase prop-
erties.55,13 Accordingly, the observation that gas
phase QM data cannot always be reproduced exactly
and still yield good agreement with condensed exper-
imental data is not surprising. It will be of interest to
see if extension of the potential energy function, most
notably to include electronic polarizability, will allow
empirical force ﬁeld calculations to adequately treat
both the gas and condensed phases.
An area of emphasis in CHARMM27 that is some-
times perceived as relatively easy to represent cor-
rectly via force ﬁelds is the internal geometries (i.e.,
bond lengths and valence angles). However, accurate
reproduction of experimental data on both the bases
and the sugar moiety56,57 can be problematic. In par-
ticular, difﬁculties arise with the sugar because of its
ﬂexibility, with its bond lengths and valence angles
differing signiﬁcantly between the north and south
puckers. Obtaining a single set of parameters that
reproduce these variations is not trivial. When devel-
oping CHARMM27 signiﬁcant efforts were devoted
to address this difﬁculty. The CHARMM27 bond
lengths in the deoxyribose and ribose moieties deviate
by approximately 0.01 Å from their counterparts in
high precision crystal structures of nucleosides and
nucleotides.29 The corresponding deviations in the
valence angles vary from 1.0° to 2.0°. In the majority
of cases, the differences for the individual bonds and
angles were less than error estimates for the experi-
mental data, though exceptions exist. The importance
of the improved representation of internal geometries
in nucleic acid force ﬁelds for the reﬁnement of ex-
perimental structures has been stressed repeated-
ly.58–61 Accordingly, use of the CHARMM27 nucleic
acid force ﬁeld in experimental structure reﬁnement
Optimization of the CHARMM27 nucleic acid param-
eters included both crystal and solution phase simu-
CHARMM Nucleic Acids Force Field
lations of ﬁve DNA duplexes and one RNA du-
plex.29,30 As these systems were part of the target data
for the optimization they do not represent independent
tests of the force ﬁeld, but they do offer an initial
validation. Results from these simulations yielded the
following observations. In high water activity simu-
lations (i.e., only enough sodium ions to neutralize the
system), the B form of DNA is favored over the A
form, consistent with experiment. RMS differences
for the EcoRI dodecamer were 4.5 and 1.9 Å with
respect to the canonical A and B forms, respectively,
showing the force ﬁeld to yield structures in solution
that are “B-like” rather than being very close to the
canonical B form. Interestingly, there is a strong an-
ticorrelation between the sampling of the A and B
forms in MD simulations of DNA in solution, indi-
cating that the force ﬁeld is sampling this equilibrium.
Such an anticorrelation is not present with RNA,
indicating that the phenomena is not an intrinsic prop-
erty of the force ﬁeld. With the RNA UAAGGAG-
GUGUA dodecamer, rms differences were 1.9 and
5.9 Å with respect to the canonical A and B forms,
respectively. Proper sampling of the equilibrium be-
tween the A and B forms of DNA was further evi-
denced by the A form being stable in its crystal
environment and in the presence of low water activity
induced by 75% ethanol in solution. The conforma-
tional space being sampled in both DNA and RNA is
in good agreement with crystal survey data for the
phosphodiester backbone, the sugar, and the glycosyl
linkage. In addition, the force ﬁeld yields helicoidal
properties that are also in good agreement with crystal
survey data. This agreement is particularly relevant
due to the helicoidal parameters not being included as
target data during parameter optimization. Finally,
hydration of both DNA and RNA is consistent with a
variety of experimental data.30
Several independent tests of the force ﬁeld have
also been performed in our laboratory. Simulations of
a ZDNA crystal (note that ZDNA was not included as
target data) yielded a RMS difference of 0.8 Å with
respect to the experimental structure. However, sev-
eral of the dihedral distributions were offset slightly
as compared to crystal survey data. Recently, we
published studies on DNA alone and bound the (cy-
tosine-5)-methyltransferase from HhaI (M.HhaI).38
Simulations of the DNA alone in aqueous solution
yielded stable structures as did the DNA–M.HhaI
complex simulations. The latter are important in that
they indicate that the force ﬁeld is appropriate for
DNA–protein studies. Other simulations yet to be
published include studies on various DNA sequences
in solution, DNA substituted with 3-phosphorami-
date, RNA, and ﬂipping of bases out of a DNA duplex
(N. Banavali and A.D. MacKerell, Jr., manuscripts in
preparation). In all cases, agreement with experiment
with respect to structure is satisfactory.
Independent tests of the force ﬁeld have also been
performed in other laboratories. As mentioned above,
Langley conﬁrmed the sensitivity of the force ﬁeld to
water activity, showing the A form of DNA to be
stabilized in the presence of 4M NaCl.36 Nilsson and
co-workers have done simulations on d(CGCGCG)2,
which yielded RMS differences approximately 2.3
and 1.1 Å from the A and B forms of DNA, respec-
tively. In another study the Cornell62 and most recent
AMBER force ﬁelds,63 CHARMM27 and the BMS36
force ﬁelds were compared (T. Cheatham, personal
communication). With CHARMM27 good results
were obtained for a variety of properties with possible
limitations in the force ﬁeld including more base
inclination than expected, large ﬂuctuations in roll,
tilt, and twist, and a relatively small amount of minor
groove width sequence dependence. Overall, it is
clear that the CHARMM27 force ﬁeld is well behaved
in a variety of conditions, although certain limitations
may be present. Future work will address these limi-
CHARMM27 all-atom force ﬁeld is appropriate for
simulations of various structural forms of DNA and
RNA as well as DNA in complexes with proteins and
lipids. This has been achieved via a very careful and
detailed parameter optimization procedure that explic-
itly examines each moiety comprising oligonucleo-
tides. However, such an approach leads to a force ﬁeld
of minimal transferability, requiring that additions to
the force ﬁeld be parametrized following the proce-
dures used for the entire force ﬁeld.
One advantage of the detailed parameter optimiza-
tion approach employed in CHARMM27 are insights
gained into the properties of DNA and RNA. Based
on the QM calculations performed to obtain local
target data a number of novel insights on the contri-
bution of the intrinsic conformational energetics of
nucleic acid moieties to DNA and RNA duplex struc-
ture have been obtained.32–34 An interesting observa-
tion was that the intrinsic energetics about the rotat-
able bonds in the phosphodiester backbone make a
signiﬁcant contribution to the structural properties
observed in duplex DNA and RNA. This observation,
which is analogous to the Ramachadran diagram used
for the peptide backbone of proteins, shows that the
low energy or allowed regions associated with rota-
MacKerell, Banavali, and Foloppe
tion about the backbone dihedrals correspond to the
regions sampled in duplex DNA and RNA. Thus, the
intrinsic conformational energetics of nucleosides and
nucleotides have a signiﬁcant impact on duplex struc-
ture. At the nucleoside level, cytosine was shown to
favor the A and Z forms of DNA relative to A, G, and
T, showing that base composition can inﬂuence the
equilibrium between the A, B, and Z forms of DNA.
Other observations include the experimentally known
relationship between base orientation and sugar pucker25
being dominated by base to furanose interactions, the
barrier between the north and south sugar pucker
conformations (i.e., the O4-endo pucker) involving a
decrease in sugar pucker amplitude, and differences in
sugar pucker energetics occurring in the presence of
purines versus pyrimidines. These observations may
explain differences in sugar pseudorotation angle dis-
tributions as a function of base observed in crystal
Two major directions will dominate future develop-
ment of the CHARMM27 nucleic acid force ﬁeld. The
ﬁrst will be continued optimization of the current
force ﬁeld in the context of the present form of the
potential energy function presented in Eq. (1). The
second will be the development of a new force ﬁeld
based on an extended potential energy function.
The quality of a force ﬁeld is based on its ability to
reproduce a given set of target data. For oligonucle-
otides, this target data is typically crystal structures of
DNA and RNA duplexes. However, the presence of
the lattice environment in crystals is known to impact
the structure of the oligonucleotide, especially DNA,64
limiting the extrapolation of crystal data to solution.
Other biophysical data, including x-ray diffraction of
oriented ﬁbers and vibrational spectroscopy,65–72 are
also available and increase our understanding of the
properties of oligonucleotides in solution. NMR is an
alternate method to obtain structural data on DNA;
however, with oligonucleotides, the lack of long-
range nuclear Overhauser effect (NOE) data limits the
structural details that may be obtained.54 This limita-
tion, however, is gradually being overcome via the
development of novel NMR techniques based on iso-
tope labeling and dipolar coupling data that are allow-
ing for increased resolution of NMR determined
structures.73–75 The availability of such novel infor-
mation will allow for continual reevaluation of nu-
cleic acid force ﬁelds. Such reevaluation will certainly
stimulate further optimization of the CHARMM27
force ﬁeld for nucleic acids, allowing it to more
accurately represent the experimental regimen.
The polyanionic nature of oligonucleotides makes
proper treatment of the electrostatic interactions es-
sential for successful modeling work. This is evi-
denced by the need to treat electrostatics via either
PME or atom truncation methods with smoothing and
extended cutoffs in order to obtain stable structures in
solution during MD simulations. The next obvious
advance toward improved treatment of electrostatics
is inclusion of electronic polarizability in the potential
energy function. Inclusion of electrostatic polarization
will allow for more accurate treatment of interactions
in different environments, an effect that may be as-
sumed to be especially important in DNA where sig-
niﬁcant structural changes occur in response to the
environment. Work in a variety of laboratories over
the last several years has involved the development of
force ﬁelds that include electronic polarizability.76–81
These models generally employ ﬂuctuating charge or
induced dipole models to treat the polarizability. We
have recently initiated a project (A. D. MacKerell, Jr.,
and B. Roux, work in progress) on developing an
empirical force ﬁeld based on a Drude oscillator.82
While work has been initiated on polarizable force
ﬁelds, the lack of success in developing a comprehen-
sive force ﬁeld for macromolecules emphasizes the
difﬁculty in its implementation. Thus, while the avail-
ability of polarizable force ﬁelds is expected to lead to
improvements in the modeling of nucleic acids, it may
take several more years until a fully functional polar-
izable force ﬁeld for nucleic acid is ready for general
The CHARMM27 nucleic acid force ﬁeld represents
over 15 years of continuous development during
which the force ﬁeld has evolved from an in vacuo
model to its present form. This evolution has been
stimulated by increasing computational resources and
improved simulation algorithms leading to the use of
models with explicit solvent representations. Knowl-
edge gained from explicit solvent MD simulations on
the behavior of biomolecular force ﬁelds has led to the
development of the parameter optimization strategies
outlined in this article. This evolution will continue in
the context of the present form of the potential energy
function as well as to an extended form that contains
terms for the treatment of electronic polarizability.
Both paths will certainly lead to further improvements
in our ability to model nucleic acids via empirical
CHARMM Nucleic Acids Force Field
Work in a variety of laboratories has shown the
CHARMM27 nucleic acid parameters to yield stable
simulations of DNA and RNA in solution using both
CHARMM27 force ﬁeld offers an effective tool for
modeling of nucleic acids in the condensed phase.
The nucleic acid force ﬁeld was also developed such
that it is compatible with the CHARMM22 protein13
and CHARMM27 lipid47 force ﬁelds. Compatibility
of these force ﬁelds will allow for mesoscopic simu-
lations of heterogeneous biomolecular systems of in-
creasing complexity.1 Examples of both protein–
DNA38 and lipid–DNA39 MD simulations using the
CHARMM force ﬁelds are present in the literature.

and D. Strahs for helpful discussions and preliminary tests
of the CHARMM27 force ﬁeld during its development.
